Post_Text: Two charter schools. Same size, same payroll, same number of teachers. One pays thousands more for workers' comp. Every single year. The difference is a number nobody ever handed you: [LINK]

Your workers' comp bill has a credit score built into it. It follows your school, it compounds across renewals, and it's frequently wrong. Nobody on your account has checked it in years. Here's how to: [LINK]

An office administrator filed under a hands-on job code years ago. Nobody revisited it. So you've been paying the higher rate on that salary every year since. Multiply that across your staff. This is how schools quietly overpay: [LINK]

Post_Text: Here's the part of workers' comp almost no school leader knows. The reserve on an open claim, not the dollars actually paid, is what inflates your premium. A claim that settles cheap can still cost you for years. Why: [LINK]

An injured employee sitting home collects wage benefits that climb, and that cost lands in your premium. The same employee back on light duty shrinks the bill. Most schools have no return-to-work plan at all. Build one: [LINK]

There's one date each year when your claim figures get pulled into next year's premium. A bloated reserve sitting open on that date freezes into your bill for years. Most schools have never heard of it. You should know yours: [LINK]

One question for whoever handles your school's insurance. When did anyone last pull your e-mod worksheet, walk your class codes against your real roster, and review your open-claim reserves before they locked in? If the answer is a shrug, read this: [LINK]
